proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store descriptions consistently frame Darwin's Paradox! as a single-player, story-driven 3D platformer-adventure with puzzle solving and light stealth. The strongest recurring gameplay signals are 'platforming sequences,' 'moments of puzzle-solving,' and verbs like swim, wall-climb, hide, infiltrate, jump, camouflage, and shoot ink to overcome traps and escape. The presentation is described as a cinematic animated-movie-like adventure in a mysterious industrial complex with sprawling handcrafted levels, which supports a linear/level-based campaign structure and a secondary cinematic action-adventure flavor. Combat is not presented as dominant; instead, stealthy avoidance, traversal, and puzzle progression appear to define the core loop, making action_platformer the best fit.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close on authored cinematic progression, environmental hazard navigation, puzzle-platforming, and minimal-combat survival through a sinister industrial project.
Strong match for linear platform-adventure structure, stealthy evasion, chase sequences, oversized threats, and puzzle-led movement, though it leans more horror than the anchor.
Escape-focused cinematic platforming through hostile industrial facilities with traps and stealth is highly relevant, though its classic 2D platformer cadence is a bit more old-school and systemic.
Good fit on cinematic campaign, puzzle-solving, light stealth, and dangerous traversal, but companion mechanics and a gentler storybook pacing make it slightly less direct.
Linear cinematic puzzle-platforming built around traps, environmental hazards, and vulnerable escape-focused progression.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Multiple stored descriptions consistently identify the game as a cozy hidden-object adventure focused on finding cats, characters, and balloons across two illustrated scenes/settings. The copy emphasizes pressure-free, family-friendly search gameplay, detailed static environments, intuitive controls, and solo play, with no indication of combat, platforming, racing, or broader action-adventure systems. Metacritic also labels the genre as Hidden Object. This strongly supports hidden_object_puzzle as the core gameplay identity.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close match in casual hidden-cats scene scanning, with charming artwork and a soothing, accessible play loop.
Similar pure hidden-object structure centered on spotting many hidden cats in static illustrated scenes for relaxed progression.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store descriptions consistently identify a first-person cooking/restaurant simulation focused on preparing dishes, managing ingredients and stations, serving guests, experimenting with recipes, and optional co-op/sandbox play. However, the allowed taxonomy archetype list does not contain a clear fit for a first-person cooking or restaurant-work simulation. Available simulation-adjacent archetypes such as management_tycoon, life_sim, farming_sim, engineering_sandbox_sim, or transport_sim would misrepresent the game's primary hands-on culinary simulation loop.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Direct predecessor with essentially the same first-person realistic cooking loop, career/sandbox structure, physics-driven kitchen interaction, and progression.

Very close on restaurant service, dish prep, customer satisfaction, kitchen workflow, and culinary progression, though it leans more into broader restaurant management than tactile first-person simulation.
Another first-person food-production sim with recipe precision, equipment use, orders, and business progression, but bakery production/delivery is a narrower and less restaurant-service-driven loop.
Shares first-person cooking tasks, recipe creation, customer orders, and food-business management, but driving and truck operation split focus away from a dedicated kitchen sim.
Restaurant sim with deep recipe creation, menu customization, ingredient choices, and progression around building a culinary identity.

title: Little Nemo and the Guardians of Slumberland

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions repeatedly and explicitly call the game a 'Metroidvania adventure' with a 'vast, non-linear world' that is 'seamlessly connected.' They describe classic side-scrolling platformer play, ability-gated progression via toys and pajamas, exploration of interconnected domains, boss fights, collectibles, and unlocking new abilities to access new pathways. That combination strongly supports metroidvania over a simpler action-platformer label.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close fit: colorful 2D metroidvania with approachable exploration, interconnected map, light combat, boss fights, and transformation-based ability gating.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: Monster Boy and the Cursed Kingdom
staged_neighbors.requested_title: Monster Boy and the Cursed Kingdom
staged_neighbors.why_similar: Excellent match on side-scrolling exploration, form/ability unlocks, bosses, secrets, and bright accessible presentation; slightly more equipment/combat driven than the anchor.
Strong retro-rooted comparison with interconnected exploration and transformation-gated progression; a bit more old-school and combat-forward, but very aligned structurally.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: Ori and the Will of the Wisps
staged_neighbors.requested_title: Ori and the Will of the Wisps
staged_neighbors.why_similar: Clear metroidvania overlap in non-linear map, upgrade gating, bosses, and platforming, but it is more demanding, more kinetic, and more combat/puzzle intense than the anchor.
Retro-styled side-scrolling adventure with strong 8-bit feel, ability-gated exploration, approachable action-platforming, and a colorful fantasy tone.

missing_must_include_titles: Monster Boy and the Cursed Kingdom
Ori and the Will of the Wisps

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify the game as a roguelite action shooter built around surviving progressively harder waves, leveling up during runs, drafting power-ups, unlocking chair upgrades, and finding build synergies. The core loop is presented as single-player or up to 4-player co-op, with PvP arena/king-of-the-hill described as an extra mode rather than the main identity. Combat is dominant, fast, and hybridized between shooting and hacking/slashing, with minibosses and bosses closing out wave progression. This strongly fits co_op_action_roguelite over arena_fps because the text emphasizes roguelite run structure, power-up drafting, co-op survival, and meta-upgrades rather than primarily match-based FPS competition.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Excellent fit on run-based co-op horde combat, scaling difficulty, bosses, item stacking, and synergy-driven builds. It is less arena-wave focused and less explicitly shooter-melee hybrid, but the core loop and audience are very close.

Very strong match on 4-player co-op, repeated roguelite runs, hero differentiation, randomized progression, and build construction. More level-based FPS than arena survival, but still a clear rail fit.

Strong overlap in fast roguelite shooter runs, upgrade drafting, bossing, and skill-heavy combat. The stricter FPS perspective, 2-player cap, and more stage-run structure make it somewhat less aligned than the top two.
4-player co-op horde shooter with chaotic swarm pressure, loadout builds, and run-to-run combat intensity in a third-person perspective.
Fast co-op arena-style horde shooter focused on class builds, loot/progression, and surviving dense enemy waves.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently frame Subliminal as a first-person psychological horror experience centered on exploring handcrafted open-ended memory-spaces and solving light/perspective-based environmental puzzles. The Steam detailed description emphasizes hallucination-like instability, unsettling spaces, a pursuing inhuman figure, and psychologically terrifying encounters, which strongly supports psychological_horror over action_horror or survival_horror. Gameplay identity appears to be exploration plus puzzle-solving under threat rather than combat-driven play; chase/pressure sequences justify light survival-oriented encounter framing, but not dominant combat. Metacritic explicitly labels it 'First-Person Adventure,' while Steam copy repeatedly stresses psychological horror, environmental puzzle solving, and single-player progression through authored spaces.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Closest mechanical comp: first-person surreal puzzle design centered on forced perspective and reality-bending spatial logic, which strongly overlaps with Subliminal’s core puzzle loop.
Very strong first-person reality-manipulation puzzle match with exploration through authored spaces; less horror-forward, but highly aligned on environment-shaping progression.

Strong first-person psychological horror comparison with unstable interiors, authored exploration, and minimal combat, though it is more of a walking-horror experience than a systems-heavy puzzle game.

Good fit on first-person psychological horror, puzzle-solving under pressure, and survival-through-avoidance, but it leans more toward direct scare design and item-use horror than perspective-space manipulation.
First-person psychological horror focused on authored exploration, surreal spaces, puzzle-solving, and pressure from hostile encounters over combat mastery.

title: Raider Kid and the Ruby Chest

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present a 2D side-scrolling exploration platformer built around ability-gated progression: the player explores ancient ruins across multiple regions, solves puzzles, finds new equipment and abilities, discovers hidden passages, and uses those upgrades to access new areas. The copy also explicitly mentions bosses and enemies, supporting moderate combat rather than pure platforming. Retro handheld presentation and a light comedic tone are clear, but the core identity is the exploration-plus-upgrade gating loop characteristic of a metroidvania rather than a linear action platformer.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close fit: 2D action-platform exploration built around acquiring pirate gear that expands combat and traversal, revisiting areas, finding secrets, and fighting bosses.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: Monster Boy and the Cursed Kingdom
staged_neighbors.requested_title: Monster Boy and the Cursed Kingdom
staged_neighbors.why_similar: Strong match on side-scrolling exploration, hidden passages, bosses, equipment upgrades, and ability-based path unlocking across an interconnected adventure.

Shares exploration, puzzle-solving, boss fights, and tool/weapon-driven progression, though it leans more toward broad action-adventure with towns and dungeons than a tighter platform-led metroidvania loop.

Good retro side-scrolling action-adventure comparison with non-linear exploration, combat upgrades, bosses, and gated progression, but it skews more action-RPG and less puzzle-platform compactness than the anchor.
Side-scrolling exploration with tool-based progression, region backtracking, secrets, and combat-driven upgrade gating.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored Steam copy gives strong gameplay evidence: this is an arcade-style coin pusher built around repeated runs, score-chasing, and physics-driven coin drops, with roguelike deckbuilding layered on top through collectible special coins, 150 items, character-specific loadouts/playstyles, endless mode, and multiple difficulty levels. It is not literal pinball, but within the available taxonomy the closest fit is pinball because the core interaction is a non-combat arcade table/machine focused on chaining effects, manipulating a physical playfield, and maximizing payouts/scores. Secondary card_battler reflects the explicit 'roguelike deckbuilder' structure, though card play itself is absent, so it remains secondary rather than primary.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Physics-driven pachinko turns are wrapped in a roguelike run structure with relic synergies and build planning.
Another roguelite built around a gambling-machine format, with symbol combo stacking and run-defining synergies.
Shares the dopamine-heavy roguelike deckbuilder loop of assembling escalating score combos from item and ruleset interactions.
Arcade physics play is blended with roguelike progression, making moment-to-moment shots and build choices both central.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present Tombwater as a '2D Top-Down Souls-Like' with punishing combat, 20+ boss fights, an interconnected world, weapons/spells/charms, crafting, quests, and exploration of optional areas. The strongest gameplay identity is therefore soulslike_action_rpg rather than metroidvania: the game is explicitly top-down rather than side-scrolling, and the copy emphasizes boss-centric challenging combat, build/loadout variety, and soulslike structure more than platforming. Metroidvania remains a strong secondary influence because the store text explicitly cites 'Metroidvania elements' and an interconnected world with secret optional areas.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Explicitly an isometric souls-like action RPG with challenging combat, heavy boss focus, character growth, quests, upgrades, and grim exploration; this is the closest gameplay match.
Very close on top-down perspective, technical dodge/parry combat, bosses, secrets, and intricately connected world structure, though it is more puzzle-adventure than build-focused soulslike RPG.
Top-down combat-centric action RPG with punishing encounters, bosses, ranged/magic/melee mix, upgrades, and secrets; slightly less soulslike/system-build heavy than the best fits.
Strong match on top-down 2D action RPG combat, upgrades, branching paths, and discovery, but it is less explicitly soulslike and less boss-centric in structure.
Top-down action RPG with interconnected exploration, ability gating, tough boss fights, and flexible melee/ranged builds.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored DB evidence is sufficient to accept this as a visual novel. Metacritic explicitly labels the game's genre as 'Visual Novel,' which is strong direct evidence. The descriptive copy is entirely story- and character-focused, emphasizing Nobushige Sanada, the Ten Braves, their bonds, and a historical Sengoku-era narrative aimed at otome players. No concrete evidence supports action, strategy, simulation, or combat-driven play, and the current audit already flags combat_presence as none plus authored_linear and historical signals. Together this supports a single-player, non-combat, historically themed visual novel identity.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Extremely close fit: historical Japanese otome visual novel, famous samurai-era cast, branching romance routes, and heavy story focus with minimal non-VN gameplay.
Very strong match on war-era Japanese otome VN identity, faction conflict, route-based progression, and character-affection-driven endings.
Description strongly matches a Sengoku-era otome visual novel about ninja loyalties and romance routes; noisy taxonomy data weakens confidence slightly, but gameplay identity still fits well.
Good otome VN comp with period Japan setting and choice-led narrative structure, but the supernatural/demonic angle makes it a bit less aligned with the anchor's historical-war focus.
Historical Japanese otome VN with route-based romance, samurai-era political turmoil, and minimal gameplay beyond reading and choices.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently and repeatedly identify GRIME II as an 'action-adventure metroidvania' and describe hallmark genre structure: side-scrolling exploration through a deep interconnected world, secret paths gated by newly acquired forms/abilities, platforming traversal, and combat used both for progression and discovery. Combat is a major pillar, with parry/grasp mechanics, enemy absorption, summoned molds, environmental combat, special attacks, and epic bosses, which also supports a soulslike-leaning secondary identity. The world is presented as a handcrafted surreal space with distinct locations and discovery emphasis. Evidence is strong enough to accept metroidvania as the primary gameplay archetype.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Direct predecessor with the same core metroidvania structure, weighty combat, parry/absorb mechanics, and build-driven progression.
Combat-forward 2D metroidvania with deliberate melee play, traversal-gated exploration, bosses, and strong build/weapon identity.
Benchmark interconnected metroidvania focused on exploration, tough boss fights, ability unlocks, and precise side-scrolling combat.
Shares the soulslike 2D action-RPG feel: stamina-minded melee combat, character builds, oppressive world design, and demanding encounters.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: ENDER LILIES: Quietus of the Knights
staged_neighbors.requested_title: Ender Lilies: Quietus of the Knights
staged_neighbors.why_similar: Another dark action metroidvania built around collecting defeated foes' powers for combat and exploration upgrades.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: The stored descriptions provide solid high-level identity: a single-player hand-drawn noir time-loop murder mystery set in 1937 Rio, centered on learning character schedules, linking evidence on a Thinking Board, reconstructing a timeline, manipulating routines, and solving the case through logic. However, the evidence does not cleanly map to any allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype. It strongly suggests investigative adventure / detective puzzle design, but there is no explicit support for the required loops of hidden_object_puzzle (hidden-object scenes or point-and-click object hunt), visual_novel, immersive_sim_action, or other available archetypes. Because the gameplay identity is clear yet sits outside the current archetype list, classification should be rejected rather than forced into a mismatched bucket.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Knowledge-driven time-loop investigation where each reset helps you map routines, uncover contradictions, and solve a larger mystery.
Time-loop mystery focused on observing character schedules and intervening at the right moments to change deadly outcomes.
Open-ended detective structure with suspect interviews, evidence gathering, timeline reconstruction, and making your own accusation.
Logic-heavy investigation centered on linking clues, reconstructing events, and identifying the truth through deduction rather than combat.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: The Case of the Golden Idol
staged_neighbors.requested_title: The Case of the Golden Idol
staged_neighbors.why_similar: Pure deduction mystery that asks you to study scenes, connect evidence, and infer what happened with minimal hand-holding.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence clearly describes a single-player, procedurally generated run-based top-down shooter/roguelike with a distinctive hybrid structure: turn-based tactical planning blended with real-time bullet-hell action, plus build synergies, unlockable characters, weapons, and missions. However, the allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype list does not contain a good-fit archetype for a single-player tactical/bullet-hell roguelike shooter of this kind. Available shooter archetypes such as arena_fps, military_fps, tactical_shooter, hero_shooter, loot_shooter, or extraction_shooter do not match the top-down run-based roguelike identity, and turn_based_tactics would also be misleading because the game is explicitly a shooter with hybrid real-time bullet-hell combat rather than primarily overhead squad tactics. Because the gameplay evidence is strong but does not map cleanly to an allowed archetype, rejection is the safest taxonomy outcome.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close on turn-based movement-driven combat, top-down ranged encounters, procedural runs, build/perk progression, and sci-fi roguelike structure; less bullet-hell and less action-shooter feel than the anchor, but an excellent gameplay comp.

Shares the anchor's signature time-moves-when-you-do combat logic and deliberate projectile-dodging/shooting rhythm, but differs substantially in first-person perspective, level structure, and lack of roguelike run/build identity.

Strong match for top-down bullet-hell roguelite shooting, room-based runs, weapon variety, and dodge-heavy combat, but it is fully real-time and lacks the anchor's tactical move-linked temporal structure.

Good fit on top-down run-based shooter combat, randomized progression, weapon handling, and build synergies, but its fully real-time intensity and co-op-leaning roguelite identity make it a looser match.
Run-based top-down combat where enemy actions are tightly linked to your movement timing, emphasizing deliberate positioning over constant real-time firing.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently and explicitly define the game as a tough-as-nails 3D platformer centered on 'straight forward twitch reflex platforming' and 'precision platforming action.' The text emphasizes brutal but fair levels, repeated deaths from hazards, wall-leaping, and escalating difficulty, which strongly matches precision_platformer. Metacritic genre labeling supports third-person 3D platforming, while the copy's references to 'a bunch of levels,' Dark World levels, and boss fights support a level-based campaign structure with boss-centric encounters. Combat is not described as a primary loop, so the game fits precision platformer far better than action_platformer or 3d_collectathon; unlockable secrets are present, but collection is not framed as the core objective.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Direct predecessor and clearest benchmark: same hazard-dense, mastery-heavy, instant-retry precision platforming loop, just in 2D rather than 3D.
Extremely strong precision-platforming comp with exact movement, rapid respawns, handcrafted challenge rooms, and repeated-death mastery, though it remains 2D and more narrative-driven.
One of the better 3D movement-skill comps, especially for trap-heavy platforming gauntlets, but combat, exploration, quests, and progression are much more central than in the anchor.
Shares level-based 3D platforming, demanding jumps, replayable stages, and boss encounters, but it is a broader action-platformer with less of the pure precision-death loop identity.
Third-person 3D platforming built around tight movement, hazard-heavy stages, short challenge courses, and precision-first level clears.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently and explicitly identify the game as a 'fast, 2D action platformer' centered on vertical movement, fluid combat, and skill-driven play. Multiple sources reinforce side-scrolling/2D platforming, while the Steam detailed description adds concrete combat signals: chaining melee strikes, evasive dashes, supernatural attacks, and level-based environmental challenges/puzzles flowing into combat. The copy also supports a linear campaign-like single-player structure, dark fantasy setting, pixel art presentation, and high-execution platforming/action focus. Evidence is strong and internally consistent for action_platformer rather than metroidvania or a pure platformer.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close on level-based 2D action-platforming, combat-platforming interplay, dark fantasy framing, and multiple playable characters with distinct abilities.
Excellent match on brutal 2D action-platforming, stage-like progression, hazards, bosses, and high-intensity combat, though it is more gun-focused and less character-swapping driven.

Strong overlap in 2D combat-platforming and on-the-fly character switching, but its castle exploration, unlock-based routing, and metroidvania progression are looser fits for a clearly level-based anchor.
Good fit for fast side-scrolling combat and acrobatic traversal, but its later shift into more expansive exploration and its lighter tone move it away from the anchor's apparent structure and feel.
Fast 2D stage-based action-platforming with combo-driven melee combat, evasive mobility, and a gothic supernatural tone.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Hozy as a cozy restoration/decorating simulation focused on cleaning, painting, furnishing, and interacting with objects across nine restoreable locations, with no timers, scores, penalties, or combat. The evidence is strong for a non-combat cozy renovation sim loop, but the current Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype set does not provide a clear fit for this gameplay identity. It is not well-supported as life_sim, management_tycoon, hidden_object_puzzle, or any action/adventure/RPG/shooter/racing archetype. Because the gameplay is clear yet falls outside the available archetypes, this should be rejected rather than forced into a poor match.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close match on first-person cleanup, painting, furnishing, and creative restoration of spaces; sandbox/build options and co-op broaden it, but the core loop strongly overlaps.
Core renovation fantasy is extremely close: clean, paint, repair, install, and decorate interiors with satisfying tools and visual transformation.
Shares the restoration structure, first-person cleanup/repair flow, and light decoration, though it is more task-driven and less homey/cozy than Hozy.
Excellent match for soothing, tactile cleaning and no-pressure progression, but it lacks Hozy's paint-and-furnish interior design loop.
First-person interior renovation and furnishing loop with cleaning, painting, placing decor, and shaping cozy spaces.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored Steam copy gives clear gameplay identity: a single-player narrative RPG where you recruit companions, level abilities, make consequential choices, and engage in 'tactical turn-based combat' with an 'interesting twist on strategic, turn-based vehicular battles.' The text repeatedly emphasizes crew management, tactical encounters, mystery-solving in an alternate 1930s floating New York, and choice-driven story progression. That supports turn_based_tactics as the core play identity, with western_narrative_rpg as a secondary layer.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close on party-based, story-led RPG structure: recruited team, mission-driven progression, heavy dialogue/choice focus, and turn-based tactical combat. Setting differs, but gameplay identity is highly aligned.

Strong fit on recruited specialists, campaign progression, and strategic turn-based encounters. It is a bit more infiltration/skirmish-focused and less mystery-RPG-driven than the anchor, but still a strong rail candidate.

Good match for squad management, character builds, consequential choices, and turn-based tactical combat. It is more systems-heavy, broader, and combat-forward than Aether & Iron’s more authored mystery-narrative framing.

Strong overlap in investigation, authored narrative RPG structure, skill checks, and choice-driven progression. It falls short on the anchor’s party-tactics identity, so it fits better as an adjacent narrative comp than a top gameplay match.
Party-based turn-based tactics with recruited companions, authored story progression, skill checks, and heavy narrative RPG framing.

current_live_neighbors.title: Dragon Quest XI S: Echoes of an Elusive Age - Definitive Edition
Dragon Quest XI: Echoes of an Elusive Age

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present Island of Hearts as a fully acted first-person interactive romance centered on dialogue choices, branching outcomes, hidden storylines, and multiple endings. The dominant loop is narrative progression through choice-making and relationship outcomes rather than combat, traversal, or systemic RPG play. 'Clues, exploration, and mini games' are described as supplemental, not identity-defining. Single-player campaign structure, authored branching narrative, interpersonal romance drama, casual input demands, and non-combat gameplay strongly fit visual_novel.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close match: live-action branching romance built around conversational choices, dating routes, relationship outcomes, and replay for alternate endings.
Also an obvious comp: FMV rom-com dating structure, relationship tracking, branching scenes, and replayable romantic outcomes align tightly with the anchor.
Strong overlap in FMV choose-your-path structure and relationship-influenced branching, but it is a sci-fi thriller rather than a romance-route dating experience.
Recognizable live-action branching narrative with many decisions and multiple endings, though its timed crime-thriller flow is less romance/dialogue-route focused than the anchor.
FMV romantic comedy with choice-driven progression, relationship outcomes, and a light minigame layer.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently frame MARVEL MaXimum Collection as a retro compilation/archive containing multiple distinct legacy Marvel games and versions across arcade, 8-bit, 16-bit, and portable platforms. The text explicitly says it is 'spanning multiple genres' and then lists materially different included games and modes: beat-'em-up action (X-Men: The Arcade Game), a platforming variant of Captain America and The Avengers, co-op side-scrolling action in Maximum Carnage/Separation Anxiety, and an infamously difficult shooter in Silver Surfer. Because the package aggregates several different gameplay identities rather than presenting one dominant, unified core loop, there is not a single taxonomy archetype that can be supported strongly enough. Mode labels like co-op, multiplayer, and single-player are also compilation-wide and do not resolve the gameplay identity problem.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles: The Cowabunga Collection
staged_neighbors.requested_title: Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles: The Cowabunga Collection
staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close match as a licensed retro collection spanning arcade/console eras with multiple classic game types, couch/online play for some titles, regional/version variants, rewind/save features, and museum-style bonus content.

Another legacy licensed compilation built around multiple classic versions of recognizable 16-bit games with save features, display options, and version comparison/value as a package rather than a single core loop.

Excellent fit on archival and interactive-history framing, multi-platform classic game curation, and documentary/preservation extras, though it is broader and less specifically licensed-action focused than the anchor.

Reasonable match as a modern rerelease set of multiple 8/16-bit licensed classics with save states and QoL updates, but it appears narrower in scope and less robust as an archive/museum-style package.
Licensed retro compilation built around preservation, multiple classic versions, and archival extras more than a single gameplay archetype.

title: Dying Light: The Beast Restored Land

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored official descriptions consistently identify this as a long-form open-world Dying Light campaign focused on exploration of a large handcrafted region, first-person melee combat against infected, grounded parkour traversal, scavenging/loot, day-night survival pressure, authored story progression, and optional shared-progression co-op. Restored Land is described as an enhanced version of the same core game with harsher survival rules, permanent world consequences, and one-life options, which intensify survival elements without changing the underlying gameplay identity. That supports open_world_action_adventure as the primary archetype, with strong action_horror and exploration_survival_adventure traits.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: This is the base game for the anchor's enhanced edition, with effectively the same open-world first-person parkour, melee survival, progression, and co-op structure.
Direct series predecessor with the same core loop: first-person parkour traversal, scavenging, crafted melee combat, open-world exploration, and the signature day-night threat shift.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: Dying Light 2 Stay Human: Bloody Ties
staged_neighbors.why_similar: Shares Dying Light 2's first-person freerunning, zombie combat, loot, and survival structure, but this specific candidate is the Bloody Ties DLC, a more specialized combat-story slice than the full game.

Another first-person zombie action game with visceral melee, quests, loot, progression, and co-op, though its traversal and world structure are less parkour-driven and less aligned with Dying Light's identity.
Shares first-person zombie melee, looting, weapon modding, quests, and co-op, though traversal is less parkour-driven.
